1: The resurrection appearances take place in Galilee (Matthew's primary location, Matthew 28:7,16) rather than Jerusalem (Luke's primary focus), reflecting Matthew's Galilean-community orientation. The Great Commission (Matthew 28:18-20) is delivered on a mountain (the mountain in Galilee, Matthew 28:16), continuing the Sermon on the Mount mountain-theology: the place of Torah-giving is now the place of mission-commissioning. The universal mission to all nations (panta ta ethne) is the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 12:3) and the Isaianic servant vision (Isaiah 49:6) that Matthew has traced throughout the Gospel.
2: "And behold, there was a great earthquake, for an angel of the Lord descended from heaven and came and rolled back the stone and sat on it" — the second earthquake in Matthew's passion narrative (the first at Matthew 27:51) frames the resurrection as a theophanic event. Angels appearing at tombs or graves have OT precedents; the rolling back of the stone opens the tomb not for Jesus to exit (the narrative does not describe Jesus emerging) but for the disciples to enter and for the angel's announcement to be made. The angel sits on the stone — the seal of death become a throne.
3: "His appearance was like lightning, and his clothing white as snow" — the angelic appearance described in terms of lightning and white clothing matches the Transfiguration description (Matthew 17:2) and Daniel's vision of the divine being (Daniel 7:9; Matthew 10:6). White clothing in apocalyptic literature consistently signals heavenly origin and the presence of the divine realm breaking into the earthly. The guards' reaction (Matthew 28:4) is the standard OT response to theophany: the overwhelming of human senses before divine presence.
4: "And for fear of him the guards trembled and became like dead men" — the guards' collapse (egenēthēsan hōs nekroi, became as dead) is the standard prostration response to the divine presence in the OT (Daniel 10:8-9; Ezekiel 1:28; Revelation 1:17). The irony is inverted: the soldiers set to guard against a fake resurrection are struck down as if dead, while the genuinely dead Jesus has risen. The guard, meant to prevent the story of resurrection, becomes instead a witness to the divine power that enacted it.
5: "But the angel said to the women, 'Do not be afraid, for I know that you seek Jesus who was crucified'" — "Do not fear" (mē phobeisthe) is the standard angelic greeting in epiphany scenes (Matthew 1:20; Matthew 28:10; Daniel 10:12). The angel addresses the women specifically, distinguishing their presence (faith-motivated witness) from the guards (security detail). The identification "Jesus who was crucified" uses the perfect passive participle estaurōmenon — the crucifixion is a completed, defining event whose consequences carry forward.
6: "He is not here, for he has risen, as he said. Come, see the place where he lay" — the invitation to inspect the empty tomb is an evidentiary gesture: "come and see" is the standard invitation to witness in the Fourth Gospel (John 1:39, 46), and here it functions as the angel's authentication. "As he said" anchors the resurrection to Jesus' own predictions (Matthew 16:21; Matthew 17:23; Matthew 20:19; Matthew 26:32), which the disciples had apparently not fully processed. The empty tomb is not itself the resurrection but the physical trace of it.
7: "Then go quickly and tell his disciples that he has risen from the dead, and behold, he is going before you to Galilee; there you will see him. See, I have told you" — Galilee was predicted as the location of the resurrection appearance in Matthew 26:32 (citing Zechariah 13:7: the shepherd going before the flock). The women are commissioned as the first proclaimers of the resurrection — a detail whose social significance is noted above (27:55n): their testimony, socially restricted in Jewish legal contexts, is nonetheless what the risen Lord authorizes. The urgency of "go quickly" (tachy) is characteristic of angelic commissions.
8: "So they departed quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy, and ran to tell his disciples" — the combination of fear and great joy (meta phobou kai charas megalēs) is the characteristic emotional response to encounter with the holy in Second Temple literature: the numinous generates simultaneous terror and ecstasy. Fear acknowledges that something utterly beyond the ordinary has occurred; joy anticipates what it means. The women's running connects their response to the urgency of the angelic command.
9: "And behold, Jesus met them and said, 'Greetings!' And they came up and took hold of his feet and worshiped him" — the resurrection greeting chairete (Greetings!/Rejoice!) is the ordinary Greek salutation, but in this context its root meaning of joy is actualized: here is the ground of joy. The women "took hold of his feet" (ekratēsan autou tous podas) — physical contact with the risen body, the same gesture that will be offered to Thomas (John 20:27). Worship (prosekunēsan) — the word used for Magi (Matthew 2:11) and disciples (Matthew 14:33) — is now given to the risen Christ in person.
10: "Then Jesus said to them, 'Do not be afraid; go and tell my brothers to go to Galilee, and there they will see me'" — the risen Jesus' first words repeat the angel's commission but add a new designation: "my brothers" (tous adelphous mou) for the disciples. This is the only time in Matthew's Gospel that Jesus calls his disciples "brothers" without qualification; the resurrection has transformed the relationship. In OT context, "brothers" within a covenant bond carries the weight of family solidarity. The Galilee appointment sets up the Great Commission of Matthew 28:16-20.
11: "While they were going, behold, some of the guard went into the city and told the chief priests all that had taken place" — the soldiers' report to the chief priests rather than to Pilate (their military chain of command) requires explanation. Matthew assumes that the guard was under temporary authority of the priests (as custodians of the tomb), and reports there first. The priests are thus the first official recipients of the resurrection report — before the disciples receive Mary Magdalene's news. Their response will be suppression rather than investigation.
12: "And when they had assembled with the elders and taken counsel, they gave a sufficient sum of money to the soldiers" — a second council (the first condemned Jesus; this second bribes the witnesses to the resurrection), and a second payment of money (the first paid Judas; this pays the soldiers). Matthew constructs a structural parallel: the authorities respond to both the messianic claim and its vindication with financial corruption. "A sufficient sum" (arguria hikana) suggests the bribe was substantial — enough to overcome the soldiers' risk of military discipline.
13: "Telling them, 'Tell people, 'His disciples came by night and stole him away while we were asleep''" — the cover story the leaders circulate is the exact scenario they anticipated and sought to prevent (Matthew 27:64). The soldiers are asked to confess dereliction of duty (sleeping on watch was a capital military offense under Roman law) in exchange for the bribe. The narrative's inclusion of the cover story alongside the actual events serves an apologetic function: Matthew is aware of the competing account circulating in Jewish communities and presents it as a fabrication knowingly commissioned.
14: "And if this comes to the governor's ears, we will satisfy him and keep you out of trouble" — the promise to "satisfy" (peithoō, persuade or appease) Pilate implies financial influence over the governor as well. The chief priests' willingness to bribe both soldiers and governor illustrates the extent of the institutional corruption Matthew has been building toward; having paid to execute an innocent man (Matthew 26:15), they now pay to suppress evidence of the resurrection. The cover story requires maintaining both the soldiers' silence and Pilate's non-inquiry.
15: "So they took the money and did as they were directed. And this story has been spread among the Jews to this day" — "to this day" is the same formula used for the Field of Blood (Matthew 27:8). Both monuments — the field and the story — are living witnesses to the events Matthew has narrated. The story of the theft was the standard Jewish counter-narrative to the resurrection claim; Justin Martyr's Dialogue with Trypho (ca. 155 CE) confirms it was still circulating in the mid-second century. Matthew's "to this day" anchors his writing in a moment when the competing narratives were actively in conflict.
16: "Now the eleven disciples went to Galilee, to the mountain to which Jesus had directed them" — the "mountain" in Galilee is unspecified; Matthew's Gospel has given significant events their mountain settings (Sermon on the Mount, Matthew 5; Transfiguration, Matthew 17:1; Olivet Discourse, Matthew 24:3). The mountain is the place of divine revelation and covenant in both OT (Sinai, Zion) and Matthew's Gospel. That eleven are named — not twelve — is Matthew's quiet reminder of Judas's absence.
17: "And when they saw him they worshiped him, but some doubted" — hoi de edistasan (but some doubted) is one of the most candid admissions in the resurrection accounts. The word distazō (to waver, be uncertain) was used in 14:31 when Peter doubted on the water. The worship and doubt are simultaneous — not sequential stages — among the same group. Matthew's inclusion of the doubt is not a defect to explain away but an honest account of the disciples' empirical confrontation with something that exceeded their categories.
18: "And Jesus came and said to them, 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me'" — pasa exousia en ouranō kai epi gēs: the universal authority given to the risen Christ directly echoes Daniel 7:13-14, where the Son of Man receives "dominion and glory and a kingdom, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him." Matthew's passion narrative and resurrection are the answer to the high priest's question (Matthew 26:64): the Son of Man has now been enthroned at the right hand of Power, as Jesus predicted. The authority is given — edothē — a divine passive, by the Father.
19: "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" — "all nations" (panta ta ethnē) explicitly includes Gentiles, expanding the mission from Israel (Matthew 10:5-6) to the world. The command echoes Isaiah 42:6 and Matthew 49:6 (the Servant as "a light for the nations") and Daniel 7:14 (all nations serving the Son of Man). Baptism "in the name" (eis to onoma) is formulaic for identity transfer: the baptized are brought into the family named by Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — a Trinitarian formula at the Gospel's climax.
20: "Teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you. And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age" — the "all that I have commanded" links back to the five discourses of Matthew's Gospel — the systematic presentation of Jesus' teaching. The promise "I am with you always" (egō meth' hymōn eimi pasas tas hēmeras heōs tēs synteleias tou aiōnos) frames the entire Gospel: it fulfills the "Emmanuel — God with us" prophecy of Matthew 1:23. Matthew's Gospel opens with God-with-us as a title and closes with Christ's permanent presence as a promise — the same truth, declared and delivered.
